highlights:
from languages like Ruby and Clojure, where  ...    That mystery is PPX (PreProcessor eXtensions), and once you understand it, a huge chunk of the OCaml ecosystem suddenly makes a lot more sense. This article is my attempt to demystify PPX for people like me вЂ“ developers who want to use PPX  ...    OCaml is a statically typed language with no runtime reflection. That means you canвЂ™t do things like вЂњiterate over all  ...    available at runtime вЂ“ itвЂ™s erased during compilation. One of my biggest frustrations as a newcomer was not being able  ...    Instead, OCaml took a more minimal approach: the compiler provides just two things вЂ“ extension points and attributes вЂ“  ...    That said, OCamlвЂ™s approach feels very OCaml вЂ“ pragmatic, minimal, and trusting the ecosystem to build whatвЂ™s needed on  ...    about scope вЂ“ one`@` for the innermost node, two for the enclosing declaration, three for the whole module-level. Tip: DonвЂ™t worry about memorizing all of this upfront. In practice, youвЂ™ll mostly use`[@@deriving...]` and  ...    ## Using PPX with Dune

To use a PPX library in your project, you add it to the`preprocess` stanza in your`dune` file:  ...    a`to_string` function. ## Writing Your Own PPX (A Gentle Introduction) Most OCaml developers will never need to write a PPX, but understanding the basics helps demystify the whole system. LetвЂ™s build a very simple one. Say we want an extension`[%upcase...]` that converts a string literal to uppercase at compile time. HereвЂ™s the  ...    ## Further Reading This article covers a lot of ground, but the PPX topic is pretty deep and complex, so depending on how far you want to go you might want to read more on it. Here are some of the best resources IвЂ™ve found on PPX: - A Guide to Extension Points in OCamlвЂ“ WhitequarkвЂ™s original 2014 guide that introduced many developers to  ...    - A Guide to PreProcessor eXtensionsвЂ“ OCamlverseвЂ™s reference page with a comprehensive list of available  ...    - ppxlib Quick IntroductionвЂ“ ppxlibвЂ™s own getting-started guide. The best place to begin if you want to  ...    This article turned out to be a beast! IвЂ™ve wanted to write something on the subject for quite a while now, but IвЂ™ve  ...    me!  PPX might look intimidating at first вЂ“ all those brackets and`@` symbols can feel like line noise. But the core idea is simple: PPX generates boilerplate code from your type definitions at compile time. You annotate your types with what you want (`show`,`eq`,`yojson`,`sexp`, etc.), and the PPX rewriter produces the code youвЂ™d otherwise have to write by hand. For day-to-day OCaml programming, you really only need to know: 1. Which PPX libraries exist for common tasks (serialization, testing, pretty-printing) 2. How to add PPX libraries to your dune file with`(pps...)` 3. `[@@deriving...]` on type declarations to generate useful functions The вЂњwriting your own PPXвЂќ part is there for when you need it, but honestly most OCaml developers get by just fine  ...    ThatвЂ™s all I have for you today. Keep hacking!

highlights:
Learning OCaml
**3 minute read  ...    > For as long as IвЂ™ve been into programming IвЂ™ve been learning some new programming languages on the side. The more вЂњexoticвЂќ and вЂњnicheвЂќ they were - the better.1While my professional career was mostly focused on C, C++, Java and  ...    Prolog, Common Lisp, Scheme/Racket, Clojure, Smalltalk, Erlang, Elixir, Scala,  ...    Erlang and Haskell, but I always fail to truly master them. Anyways, for some  ...    this. After compiling a short list of appealing candidates2IвЂ™ve opted forOCaml- one of the most  ...    Why OCaml? One can argue that if Clojure, Erlang and Haskell are niche then OCaml is super niche and theyвЂ™d probably be right. OCaml has been around since 1996 and itвЂ™s unlikely that it will ever gain much traction at this point. I canвЂ™t  ...    * IвЂ™ve heard some praise about it from people I respect a lot - it particular that it was a really pragmatic multi-paradigm language with a lightning fast compiler  ...    I wonвЂ™t deny IвЂ™ve got a huge fondness for Lisps andinteractive
programming. For as long as it has existed, Clojure has been my favorite programming language and I donвЂ™t think thatвЂ™s going to change any time soon. Still, I think itвЂ™s always a  ...    `print\_endline"Hello, World!"`
```
IвЂ™ve elected to start learning OCaml using the following resources: * Real World OCaml: A free book about OCaml, thatвЂ™s considered one of the best starting points in the community. * Cornell UniversityвЂ™s course on OCaml: ItвЂ™s taught at the university, but the textbook and the video  ...    * OCamlverse: An OCaml community wiki. So far I enjoy all the resources and IвЂ™ve been making steady progress with the language. In my typical style IвЂ™ve also made a few small improvements to the existing Emacs tooling (e.g.`tuareg-mode`,`flycheck-ocaml`and`utop.el`), as I was going along. Going forward expect to read here some initial impressions and tips to get started with OCaml. IвЂ™m also planning to start digging deeper in Haskell at some point, so I can compare the languages better. WeвЂ™ll see if this time IвЂ™ll be more committed than in the past. I can tell you one thing, though - playing with OCaml is a lot of fun3. Keep learning!

results.title: How To Learn OCaml
highlights:
point for learning OCaml - a functional, type-safe, expressive, and battle-proven language
### Why OCaml?  ...    > A starting point for learning OCaml - a functional, type-safe, expressive, and battle-proven language
### Why OCaml? OCaml is an open-source**functional, statically typed**, and mature language. If you want to learn such a paradigm, OCaml is far more beginner-friendly than Haskell. OCaml has**a blazingly fast compiler**and**excellent type inference**. It offers exhaustive**pattern-matching**, which makes your programs concise and a joy to work with. IвЂ™ve come to OCaml fromReasonML, a JavaScript-like syntax, and tool-chain for OCaml. But as ReasonML*is*OCaml, you should know how OCaml works. **This guide is for people who already know at least one programming language**, not for total beginners. ### BeginnerвЂ™s Guide to OCaml Books Start by reading theblog post by Alex Clemmer. It provides an overview of novice-friendly books and tools. To make it short, he recommends**Real World OCaml**(free HTML version) and**[OCaml from the Very  ...    ### OCaml Website
The official website has a dedicated section forlearning resources.  ...    The**free text book**covers a variety of topics concerning functional programming,*and*OCaml. ### A Practical Approach To get started quickly, read the OCaml section of**Learn X in Y minutes**to get the big picture. Then head over to the**Jane Street OCaml Workshop**and clone their repository. Check out Shawn WangвЂ™s**Ocaml Speedrun!**blog post on dev.to for solutions to the workshop. Install OCaml and OPAM,install Dune (via Opam), and go through the workshop. Supplement your learnings by reading the books mentioned above or by going through the*free*MOOC**Introduction to Functional Programming in OCaml**. **Exercism.io**also offers an OCaml track.
### Editor Tools OCaml usesOpamfor package management. You have to install it and then run the following command from your  ...    Make sure to use a compatible OCaml version. The latest Ocaml version (4.09.0) doesnвЂ™t work yet;[you have to  ...    IвЂ™ll write aguide about my setup with NeoVim soon.
## Links
* Ocaml Website * BeginnerвЂ™s guide to OCaml beginnerвЂ™s guides.by Alex Clemmer * OCaml Speedrun!by Shawn swyx Wang

results.title: Getting Started with OCaml in 2021 В· Perpetually Curious Blog
highlights:
OCaml is an awesome language with many fine features. I enjoy using it immensely! Unfortunately, it suffers from a perceived weakness in how to get started. Like any new skill, there can be a learning curve. The tools are all there, but combining them for a good developer experience might seem difficult at first. Often IвЂ™ve found that the barrier for getting into a new langauge is less about the new features of that language and more about learning the tools to become productive in that language. The package managers, build tools, and editor integration of a new language can be confusing, making for an awful experience. Perhaps my opinionated guide to getting started with OCaml in 2021 will help reduce any mental blocks against trying  ...    ## Install Opam First itвЂ™s necessary to install OCaml and Opam. Opam is the default package manager for OCaml projects. Ignore the other options for now, once you know more about what you want, you can make an informed choice. For now if you speak OPAM, youвЂ™ll get the most out of the community. On Linux, use your local package manger, e.g.,`apt-get install opam` for Debian and`apt install opam` for Ubuntu. For MacOS, use homebrew`brew install opam`. IвЂ™ll assume if you run something else, you can handle looking up how to install things.  ...    I strongly recommended that you pick a single OCaml version that your project will compile against. Supporting multiple compiler versions is possible and usually not too diffcult, but it complicates the process right now. Running`opam switch list-available` will show you a long list of every possible OCaml compiler. Choose the latest  ...    ocaml-variants                         4.12.0+domains                         OCaml 4.12.0, with support for multicore  ...    ocaml-variants                         4.12.0+domains+effects                 OCaml 4.12.0, with support for multicore  ...    <><> Processing actions <><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><><>  рџђ«

    в”њв”Ђв”Ђ box.ml  ...    CLI tools require command line parsing,`Cmdliner` is a common library that implements CLI parsing. We need to add it in two places: first in the`dune-project` file, to get it installed, and then in`bin/dune`, to say where weвЂ™re using it. One small digression, when generating our project,`dune` created an`box.opam` file. This describes our project to Opam,  ...    ```
(lang dune 2.8)

(generate_opam_files true)  ...    depends: [

build: [  ...    might not get what you expect. ## Editor Tooling Finally, youвЂ™ll want to get comfy with your chosen editor. If you have a preference, you should use the native LSP support in that editor, along with installing`opam install ocaml-lsp-server`. OCaml is standardising on the LSP protocol for editor interaction. If you have no editor preference, then start with VSCode and install the  ...    Personally, IвЂ™m using Emacs with the LSP mode`eglot`, which works really nicely, along with some customisations to bind certain LSP actions to keys. I highly recommend getting into Emacs as an editor because the customisation via a fully-featured language, like Lisp, is fantastic if you live in your editor like I do. results.title: Top 10 Tips for Learning OCaml
highlights: where do you start? How do you learn OCaml effectively? Fear not, because we've got you covered. In this article, we'll share our top 10 tips for learning OCaml, from getting started with the basics to mastering advanced concepts. ## 1. Start with the Basics Before you can tackle advanced OCaml concepts, you need to have a solid understanding of the basics. Start by learning the syntax and structure of the language, including variables, functions, and data types. OCaml has a unique syntax that may take some getting used to, but once you have the basics down, you'll be able to read and write OCaml code with  ...    ## 2. Practice, Practice, Practice As with any programming language, the key to mastering OCaml is practice. Start by writing simple programs to get a feel for the language, and gradually work your way up to more complex projects. Don't be afraid to make mistakes вЂ“ that's how you learn! Use online resources like Rosetta Code and Exercism to find practice problems and solutions. ## 3. Use a Good Text Editor A good text editor can make all the difference when it comes to writing OCaml code. We recommend using an editor that  ...    The OCaml standard library contains a wealth of useful functions and modules that can save you time and effort when writing code. Take the time to learn the standard library and its modules, such as List, Array, and Printf. You'll be  ...    ## 5. Understand the Type System OCaml's type system is one of its most powerful features, but it can also be one of the most challenging to understand. Take the time to learn about OCaml's type system, including its type inference and polymorphism features. Understanding  ...    ## 6. Read Other People's Code Reading other people's code is a great way to learn new programming techniques and best practices. Look for open-source OCaml projects on GitHub and read through the code to see how other developers are using the language. You can also learn a lot by participating in online OCaml communities, such as the OCaml subreddit or the OCaml Discord server.  ...    The OCaml REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) is a powerful tool for testing and experimenting with OCaml code. Use the REPL to  ...    help you catch bugs and ensure that your code is working as expected. Use a testing framework like OUnit or Alcotest to  ...    Attending OCaml conferences and meetups is a great way to meet other OCaml developers and learn about the latest developments in the language. Check out events like the OCaml Workshop and the OCaml Users and Developers Workshop to  ...    ## 10. Have Fun! Finally, remember to have fun! Learning OCaml can be challenging at times, but it's also incredibly rewarding. Take the  ...    ## Conclusion Learning OCaml can be a challenging but rewarding experience. By following these top 10 tips, you'll be well on your way to mastering this powerful functional programming language. Remember to start with the basics, practice regularly, use a good text editor, and take the time to learn the standard library and type system. With these tips in mind, you'll be writing efficient and reliable OCaml code in no time!  ... MINUTES If youвЂ™re new to OCaml you may find the ecosystem confusing. There are a few reasons for this: - Really well suited to create a programming language but got into more general purpose programming - It's a functional programming language, which it might be different paradigm than the one you're used to - It was created long time ago and it carries some of the old-fashion methods from the past - The community has a fame of being small - OCaml has been born in academia and historically been focused in type-theory and functional programming  ...    IвЂ™m not a teacher, I donвЂ™t have 10+ years of experience in OCaml, I donвЂ™t have a PhD and more terribly I didnвЂ™t finish  ...    But often itвЂ™s very useful to learn from someone who is one step above you, and not someone who's 10 steps above you. I worked professionally in OCaml and ReasonML (a dialect of OCaml) for almost 3 years (and counting) and have been  ...    - reason: Simple, fast & type safe code that leverages the JavaScript & OCaml ecosystems  ...    ## #How to look at the OCaml ecosystem The OCaml ecosystem seems overwhelming because itвЂ™s always explained in abstract terms, minimum explanations and  ...    - Compilation modes

ocaml.org/docs/up-and-running Similarly on the first-hour from the OCaml docs, here's a quick overview of the core of the language to get you up and running. ### #Types OCaml is a statically typed functional programming language. let fn x = x + 1
